{"key_event":"World War I occupation and state breakdown","reasoning":"Compared with the constitutional high point of 1900-1909, civil society retained parties, newspapers, clerical-bazaar activism, nationalist networks, and local armed movements, but Russian repression, Majles closures, foreign occupation, famine, and fragmentation sharply reduced sustained national mobilizing capacity. The Qajar state remained fiscally and militarily weak, and wartime occupation, foreign-controlled forces, and the failed Anglo-Persian Agreement left central sovereignty and administration even more degraded despite brief reform attempts.","society_change":-1.1,"society_power":5.4,"state_change":-0.6,"state_power":2.4}
